B2.value: WORKLOAD AND STORAGE CALCULATOR — HOW TO USE

B3.value: Azure Local / Storage Spaces Direct — Capacity Planning Tool

B5.value: This workbook helps you plan storage for an Azure Local cluster using Storage Spaces Direct (S2D). Enter your hardware specs and planned workloads — the calculator tells you how much usable storage you get and whether everything fits. You do NOT need to be a storage expert.

D8.value: Go to 'Hardware Inputs'. Use the DROPDOWN MENUS to enter: node count, drive type, drives per node, and drive size. The calculator IMMEDIATELY shows your available storage in a big green number.

D9.value: Go to 'Workload Planner'. Choose your scenarios: AKS, Infrastructure VMs, Dev/Test, Backup, or Custom. For AVD, go to the 'AVD Planning' tab — all AVD inputs live there. Running counter shows remaining capacity.

D10.value: Review 'Capacity Report' and 'Volume Detail'. Print or share these with your team or customer. The report explains all the numbers and includes key concepts if anyone asks questions.

D13.value: YOUR INPUT — only cells you edit. All have dropdowns or accept numbers.

D14.value: GOOD NEWS — available capacity, passing checks. Bigger green number = better.

D15.value: CALCULATED — auto-calculated values. Do not edit.

D16.value: WARNING — footprint numbers, overcommit alerts, capacity exceeded.

D18.value: LABELS — descriptions explaining what each row means.

D21.value: WHERE YOU START. Enter cluster hardware with dropdowns. See your available capacity instantly. Shows TB vs TiB conversion for Windows Admin Center. Includes N+1 compute reminder.

D22.value: WHERE YOU PLAN VOLUMES. Scenario-based: AVD summary (linked from AVD Planning), AKS (clusters + worker nodes + PVs + data services), Infrastructure VMs, Dev/Test, Backup/Archive, Custom. Enable/disable each scenario. Running capacity counter. Compute summary tracks vCPU AND RAM demand per scenario with N+1 failover checks. Overcommit sensitivity table covers all scenarios.

D23.value: SINGLE SOURCE FOR ALL AVD. Enter ALL session host, profile, and compute settings here. Density matrix, host pool scale-out, FSLogix profile estimator, network bandwidth, gold image sizing, readiness checklist. All downstream tabs pull from this tab.

D24.value: SEPARATE SOFS CLUSTER SIZING. Hardware inputs, S2D pool calculation, FSLogix demand linked from Workload Planner (which pulls from AVD Planning), IOPS estimates, SOFS VM compute demand, readiness checklist. Use when profile storage is on a dedicated file server cluster.

D25.value: PER-VOLUME TABLE. Name, resiliency, efficiency, logical size, footprint. Yellow-highlighted TiB column = the exact value to use in WAC or PowerShell when creating volumes. QUICK-START SECTION: equal-sized volumes per node per Microsoft best practice. Ready-to-copy PowerShell commands.

D26.value: WHAT YOU SHARE. Executive summary, capacity term glossary, reserve explanation, drive sizing advisory, N+1 compute reminder. Print this for project documentation.

D27.value: COMPUTE ANALYSIS. CPU and RAM capacity vs demand by scenario (including SOFS VMs). N+1 failover analysis, session host sizing, vCPU overcommit sensitivity table. All formula-linked to Hardware Inputs and Workload Planner.

D28.value: ONE-PAGE SUMMARY. Hardware config, capacity allocation, scenario breakdown, volume creation table (TB + TiB), PowerShell commands, drive layout insight, compute analysis, best practice checks. All formula-linked — prints or shares as a snapshot.

D29.value: WHAT-IF TOOL. Same total raw capacity, different drive counts per node. Shows how using more, smaller drives reduces reserve and increases available capacity. All formulas linked to Hardware Inputs — updates automatically.

D30.value: READ IF CONSIDERING THIN. Risks, when acceptable, monitoring commands, best practices checklist.

D31.value: POST-DEPLOYMENT AUDIT. Enter the volumes you actually created (count, size, resiliency, provisioning type). Compares against recommended sizing, calculates reserve loss, and shows resiliency risk for both Fixed and Thin provisioning. Includes PowerShell diagnostic and resize commands.

D32.value: DOCUMENTATION LINKS. Microsoft Learn pages covering S2D, Azure Local, compute sizing, AVD, AKS, SOFS, and FSLogix topics.

D33.value: ADMIN ONLY. Capacity efficiency factor, infrastructure volume size, TB-to-TiB reference table. Override section for Workload Planner and SOFS Planner calculated fields. Also contains the CELL PROTECTION OVERRIDE switch.

C35.value: Why does Windows show different numbers?

D35.value: Calculator uses TB (decimal). Windows uses TiB (binary). 1 TB = 0.909 TiB. IMPORTANT: When creating volumes via PowerShell or WAC, use the yellow TiB column on the Volume Detail tab — PowerShell's 'TB' parameter is actually TiB.

D36.value: Three-way mirror = 3 copies = 33% efficiency. This is WHY data survives 2 failures. Two-way = 50%. Dual Parity = 50-80% at 4+ nodes.

C37.value: Why can't I use all my pool?

D37.value: Three deductions: Drive overhead (~8%), Reserve (~1 drive/node for auto-repair), Infrastructure volume (~250 GB for Azure Local system).

D38.value: Options: fewer VMs, Two-Way instead of Three-Way where OK, more/smaller drives (see Drive Layout Comparison tab), more nodes, Dual Parity for archives.

D39.value: Production: NO, use Fixed. Thin risks pool exhaustion = VMs crash. OK for dev/test and FSLogix with monitoring. See Thin Provisioning tab.

D40.value: NVMe+SSD: NVMe becomes CACHE (not capacity). Only SSD counts. All-NVMe: all drives are capacity. Most common for new Azure Local deployments.

D41.value: Yes. New drives auto-join pool. Adding 3rd node unlocks three-way mirror. But sizing correctly upfront is simpler than expanding later.

D42.value: Size VMs to fit on N-1 nodes. If 1 node goes down, all VMs must failover to remaining nodes. This is CPU/RAM, separate from storage.

C45.value: Start with Hardware Inputs before planning workloads. Know what you have before planning what to put on it.

C46.value: Use dropdown menus — don't type resiliency types manually. Typos break formulas.

C47.value: Include ALL disks when estimating VM sizes (OS + data disks). Typical Windows Server = 80 GB OS + 100 GB data = 180 GB.

C48.value: For FSLogix, start at 5 GB/user if no data. Measure after 30 days and adjust.

C49.value: Keep pool utilization below 70%. Above 70% = limited rebuild headroom. Above 90% = danger zone.

C50.value: Consider Dual Parity for backup/archive on 4+ node clusters: 50-80% efficiency vs 33% for three-way mirror.

C51.value: Print the Capacity Report tab for project documentation.

C52.value: ⚠️ TB vs TiB: This calculator uses TB (decimal). Windows/PowerShell/WAC use TiB (binary). 1 TB ≈ 0.909 TiB. When creating volumes, ALWAYS use the yellow-highlighted TiB column on the Volume Detail and Final Report tabs. Never copy the TB column into PowerShell or WAC.

B2.value: WORKLOAD AND STORAGE CALCULATOR — Azure Local

B5.value: INSTRUCTIONS: Enter values in BLUE cells (dropdowns provided). Everything else auto-calculates.

E8.value: Physical servers in the Azure Local cluster.

E9.value: All-NVMe: all drives are capacity. NVMe+SSD: NVMe is CACHE only.

E10.value: Drives per node used for CAPACITY. Min 4 drives or more recommended

E11.value: Vendor-advertised drive size in DECIMAL TB. See Rosetta Stone below to verify against Get-PhysicalDisk.

E12.value: NVMe cache drives per node. These do NOT add capacity.

E13.value: RECOMMENDED: Fixed. See Thin Provisioning tab for risks.

E17.value: Cache drives do NOT count toward capacity.

E19.value: After NVMe overhead (~8%). See Advanced Settings tab.

E20.value: All capacity drives pooled after overhead.

E22.value: System volume created during deployment (~250 GB).

E24.value: Pool minus reserve minus infra = what you can use. See Volume Detail for per-volume TiB sizing.

E26.value: ⚠️ Windows uses TiB (binary). This is the number WAC displays. When creating volumes, use TiB values from the Volume Detail tab (yellow column).

B28.value: TB vs TiB ROSETTA STONE — Why The Numbers Don't Match

E29.value: What To Do With This Number

E30.value: ← Enter this number in C11 above. This is what this calculator uses.

E31.value: ← Windows LIES about the label. This is TiB but Windows calls it "TB".

E32.value: ← Same as PowerShell. WAC shows binary values labeled as "TB".

E33.value: ← USE THIS when creating volumes. From yellow column on Volume Detail tab.

E34.value: ← Type this in PowerShell. PS "TB" operator = binary (TiB). Yes, it's confusing.

B36.value: 🔑 RULE: Dell number → Calculator input (C11).   Yellow column from Volume Detail → WAC / PowerShell.   Never mix them.

E39.value: Physical cores per server (check CPU spec).

E42.value: Reserved for Hyper-V host OS, agents, S2D. Typical: 16-32 GB.

E45.value: Logical cores available per node for VMs.

B3.value: Plan storage by workload type — AVD, AKS, Infrastructure, Dev/Test, and more

F5.value: ⚠️ Volume sizes below are in TB (decimal). See Volume Detail tab for the TiB values to use when creating volumes in WAC or PowerShell.

B9.value: ➡ All AVD inputs have moved to the AVD Planning tab.

F9.value: Click AVD Planning tab for all session host, profile, and compute inputs.

B10.value: Number of AVD session host VMs

F44.value: Set to No to skip this scenario.

F46.value: AKS Arc clusters on this Azure Local instance.

F48.value: All worker nodes across all node pools.

F51.value: Total PVC storage for apps. Varies by workload.

F52.value: SQL MI, PostgreSQL, etc. via Arc Data Services.

F59.value: Size based on workload. Typical: 4-16 vCPU.

F60.value: Size based on workload. Typical: 16-64 GB.

F63.value: Physical cores needed after vCPU overcommit.

F66.value: DCs, SQL, file servers, management VMs, etc.

F68.value: OS + data disks combined. Typical: 150-300 GB.

F99.value: Set to No to skip this scenario.

F106.value: Set to No to skip this scenario.

C110.value: =IF(C109=0,0,ROUND(C109/IF(C108="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C108="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C108="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C108="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3)))),2))

B118.value: S2: AKS on Azure Local — Footprint

F124.value: Must be LESS than available pool.

F125.value: Logical capacity you get to use.

F126.value: =IF(MOD(C126,'Hardware Inputs'!C8)=0,"Volume count is multiple of node count.","Tip: Microsoft recommends multiple of node count ("&'Hardware Inputs'!C8&").")
F126.computed: Volume count is multiple of node count.

F132.value: Total logical vCPU across all nodes.

F133.value: Total usable RAM (after host OS reserve).

F139.value: 1:1 overcommit — SOFS VMs are not overcommitted

F141.value: Must be LESS than cluster vCPU.

F142.value: Must be LESS than cluster vCPU (with overcommit applied).

F143.value: Must be LESS than cluster RAM.

B148.value: N+1 FAILOVER CHECK (1 node in maintenance)

B161.value: Note: Hyper-V supports vCPU overcommit (standard for AVD). Memory is NOT overcommitted — Hyper-V physically reserves full RAM per VM.

B2.value: AVD PLANNING — SINGLE SOURCE FOR ALL AVD CONFIGURATION

B3.value: All AVD inputs are entered here. Workload Planner, Compute Report, Final Report, Volume Detail, and SOFS Planner all pull from this tab.

F6.value: Set to No to skip AVD entirely. All downstream tabs respect this flag.

B9.value: Number of AVD session host VMs

F9.value: Total session host VMs across all host pools.

F11.value: Additional data disks. Set to 0 if none.

D12.value: =CONCATENATE(TEXT(IF(C12="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C12="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C12="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C12="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3)))),"0%")," eff.")

F12.value: =IF(C12=IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=3,"Three-Way Mirror","Two-Way Mirror"),"","Note: "&'Hardware Inputs'!C8&"-node cluster default = "&IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=3,"Three-Way Mirror","Two-Way Mirror"))

C14.value: =IF(C13=0,0,ROUND(C13/IF(C12="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C12="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C12="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C12="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3)))),2))

F17.value: S2D = local cluster volume. SOFS = dedicated file server (see SOFS Planner). External/Azure = 0 pool footprint.

F18.value: All users who have a profile container.

F20.value: Start at 5 GB if unknown. Measure after 30 days. See Section 3 estimator below.

D22.value: =IF(C17<>"S2D Volume (local)","n/a",CONCATENATE(TEXT(IF(C22="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C22="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C22="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C22="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3)))),"0%")," eff."))

C24.value: =IF(OR($C$6="No",C17<>"S2D Volume (local)"),0,IF(C23=0,0,ROUND(C23/IF(C22="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C22="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C22="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C22="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3)))),2)))

F24.value: If profiles on external/cloud storage, footprint = 0 in pool.

F28.value: Typical: 4-8 vCPU per session host. Size per VM SKU.

F29.value: Typical: 16-32 GB per session host VM.

F30.value: vCPU overcommit. 1:1 = no overcommit. 4:1 = 4 vCPU share 1 physical core. Hyper-V supports vCPU overcommit; memory cannot be overcommitted.

F31.value: Allocation per concurrent user. Light=1, Medium=2, Power=4.

F32.value: Physical RAM per user. Light=2, Medium=4, Power=8 GB.

F33.value: Min of vCPU and RAM constraints per host.

F34.value: Concurrent users ÷ users per host (rounded up).

C35.value: =IF($C$6="No","",IF(C9>=C34,"✓ PASS — "&C9&" VMs ≥ "&C34&" needed","✗ FAIL — Need "&C34&" VMs but only "&C9&" configured"))

F36.value: Session host VMs × vCPU per host.

F37.value: Total physical RAM needed. Hyper-V reserves full RAM per VM (no overcommit).

F38.value: Physical cores needed after vCPU overcommit ratio applied.

C41.value: =IF($C$6="No","",IF(C38<='Hardware Inputs'!C47,"✓ PASS — "&C38&" of "&'Hardware Inputs'!C47&" vCPU ("&ROUND(C38/'Hardware Inputs'!C47*100,0)&"%)","✗ FAIL — Need "&C38&" but only "&'Hardware Inputs'!C47&" available"))

F41.value: AVD effective vCPU (after overcommit) vs total cluster vCPU.

C42.value: =IF($C$6="No","",IF(C37<='Hardware Inputs'!C48,"✓ PASS — "&C37&" of "&'Hardware Inputs'!C48&" GB ("&ROUND(C37/'Hardware Inputs'!C48*100,0)&"%)","✗ FAIL — Need "&C37&" GB but only "&'Hardware Inputs'!C48&" GB available"))

F42.value: AVD total RAM vs cluster RAM. Full reservation required (no overcommit).

C43.value: =IF($C$6="No","",IF(C38<='Hardware Inputs'!C49,"✓ PASS — "&C38&" of "&'Hardware Inputs'!C49&" vCPU ("&ROUND(C38/'Hardware Inputs'!C49*100,0)&"%)","⚠ WARNING — Need "&C38&" but N+1 only has "&'Hardware Inputs'!C49&" vCPU"))

C44.value: =IF($C$6="No","",IF(C37<='Hardware Inputs'!C50,"✓ PASS — "&C37&" of "&'Hardware Inputs'!C50&" GB ("&ROUND(C37/'Hardware Inputs'!C50*100,0)&"%)","⚠ WARNING — Need "&C37&" GB but N+1 only has "&'Hardware Inputs'!C50&" GB"))

F44.value: RAM with 1 node down. Full reservation (no overcommit).

B47.value: Shows maximum users per host at different vCPU/user ratios, using your inputs above.

B60.value: How many session hosts you need for your concurrent user target.

C70.value: =IF(C69<=C9,"✅ Yes (≤"&C9&" VMs)","❌ No — need "&C69&" but only "&C9&" planned")

B73.value: Estimates total profile volume size based on user type mix. Adjust the mix to model your environment. Compare with your input in Section 1 (C20).

B78.value: Power User (CAD, dev tools, large cache)

D80.value: =IF(ABS(C80-C20)<1,"✅ Matches your input (±1 GB)","⚠️ Your input uses "&C20&" GB")

B86.value: Estimates peak network bandwidth for AVD sessions. Microsoft recommends 1.5 Mbps per single-monitor session, 3+ Mbps for multi-monitor.

D92.value: =IF(C92>10000,"⚠️ Exceeds 10 GbE per node",IF(C92>1000,"⚠️ Exceeds 1 GbE per node","✅ Within 1 GbE per node"))

B95.value: Typical OS disk sizes for session hosts. Your values are set in Section 1 above.

B102.value: 💡 Tip: Use VHDX deduplication or shared image galleries to reduce per-host OS footprint when running identical gold images.

B105.value: Automated checks against your inputs and Hardware Inputs settings.

D110.value: =IF(C17="S2D Volume (local)","Profiles on cluster storage","Profiles on external storage — no S2D capacity used")

D112.computed: Weighted avg: 6.1 GB vs input: 5 GB

B2.value: SOFS Planner — Separate File Server Cluster for FSLogix Profiles

B3.value: Sizes a dedicated SOFS cluster (separate from compute cluster) for hosting FSLogix profile containers via CA SMB shares backed by S2D.

F6.value: Set to No to skip SOFS entirely. All downstream tabs respect this flag.

F9.value: Minimum 2 nodes for SOFS + cloud witness. Microsoft supported production model.

F10.value: NVMe or SSD recommended for FSLogix IOPS. Same count per node required.

F11.value: Auto-calculated from profile demand. Drive size = pool needed ÷ (nodes × drives × resiliency eff × cap eff - 1 drive reserve).

F13.value: Linked from AVD Planning C22. Change profile resiliency there.

F14.value: S2D filesystem overhead. Default 92% (same as compute cluster).

F15.value: Single = 1 VHD per user (profile+Office combined). Split = 3 VHDs per user (profile + Office/ODFC + other). Split triples IOPS and volume count.

B16.value: SOFS volume resiliency on main cluster

F16.value: Resiliency for the SOFS VM storage volumes on the Azure Local compute cluster. Independent from SOFS internal resiliency.

F20.value: Raw × resiliency efficiency × capacity efficiency factor.

F21.value: S2D reserves capacity of largest drive for rebuild. Deducted from raw.

F22.value: Usable minus drive reserve. This is what you can allocate to FSLogix volumes.

F25.value: Linked from Workload Planner C17. SOFS tab relevant when NOT "S2D Volume (local)".

F30.value: Logical demand ÷ resiliency efficiency. This is the actual pool space consumed.

F31.value: Available minus footprint. Negative = need more drives/nodes.

F32.value: Keep under 80% for S2D performance headroom.

F35.value: Typical FSLogix steady state: ~10 IOPS per active session.

F36.value: Peak during login/logoff: ~50 IOPS per user (VHD mount/dismount).

F38.value: % of users logging in simultaneously during peak (e.g., 9 AM). 10-30% typical.

F40.value: Steady users × steady IOPS + storm users × storm IOPS. Plan storage to handle this.

F43.value: Typical SOFS VM: 4-8 vCPU. Depends on concurrent SMB sessions.

F44.value: Typical SOFS VM: 8-16 GB. More for large concurrent profile mounts.

F45.value: Equals SOFS node count. Each node = 1 VM on the Azure Local cluster.

F46.value: Total vCPU consumed on main cluster by SOFS VMs.

F47.value: Total RAM consumed on main cluster by SOFS VMs.

B51.value: Capacity: SOFS can hold all profiles?

B54.value: Separate cluster: Not sharing with VM compute?

F54.value: MS does not support SOFS + VM workloads on same cluster for production.

B57.value: ⚠️ Microsoft does NOT support running SOFS and VM workloads on the same Azure Local cluster for production.

B58.value: ✅ Supported: Dedicated SOFS cluster (2+ nodes) with its own S2D pool, serving CA SMB shares for FSLogix profiles.

B59.value: ℹ️ SOFS exposes continuously available (CA) SMB shares — node failovers are transparent to FSLogix sessions.

B60.value: ℹ️ FSLogix profile containers default to 30 GB max per VHD(X). Use Redirections.xml to exclude caches (Teams, browser) and keep profiles lean.

B61.value: ℹ️ For alternative storage options (Azure Files, ANF, NAS), set the profile storage location in Workload Planner and this tab becomes informational.

B3.value: Use this table when creating volumes in Windows Admin Center or PowerShell. Copy volume names and sizes directly.

D5.value:
Calculator Only
(TB — NOT for
WAC or PowerShell)

H6.value: =IF(C6="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C6="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C6="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C6="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3))))

H7.value: =IF(C7="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C7="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C7="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C7="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3))))

H8.value: =IF(C8="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(C8="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(C8="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),IF(C8="Nested Two-Way Mirror",0.25,1/3))))

B28.value: ⚠️ IMPORTANT: Windows, PowerShell, and WAC use TiB (binary), not TB (decimal). When creating volumes, ALWAYS use the yellow highlighted column. The "-Size" parameter in PowerShell interprets "TB" as TiB. 1 TB ≈ 0.909 TiB — using the wrong column means your volumes will be ~10% larger than intended, wasting pool capacity.

B29.value: ="Microsoft recommends volume count as a multiple of your node count ("&'Hardware Inputs'!C8&"). When running New-Volume in PowerShell, the -Size parameter uses binary (TiB) even though it says TB. Use the yellow column values."
B29.computed: Microsoft recommends volume count as a multiple of your node count (3). When running New-Volume in PowerShell, the -Size parameter uses binary (TiB) even though it says TB. Use the yellow column values.

B32.value: ="Microsoft best practice: 1 volume per node (up to 4 nodes). Your " & 'Hardware Inputs'!C8 & "-node cluster = " & 'Hardware Inputs'!C8 & " equal volumes."
B32.computed: Microsoft best practice: 1 volume per node (up to 4 nodes). Your 3-node cluster = 3 equal volumes.

E35.value: =IF(C35=0,0,MIN(64,ROUNDDOWN('Advanced Settings'!E35*IF(D35="Three-Way Mirror",1/3,IF(D35="Two-Way Mirror",0.5,IF(D35="Dual Parity",IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=16,0.8,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=9,0.75,IF('Hardware Inputs'!C8>=7,2/3,0.5))),1/3)))/C35,2)))

B110.value: Note: More drives per node reduces reserve but costs more and uses more physical slots. Calculated drive sizes are theoretical — actual drives come in standard sizes (1.6, 3.2, 3.84, 7.68 TB).

C113.computed: Microsoft recommends volume count as a multiple of your node count (3) for balanced data distribution across nodes.

C114.value: ="Your current volume count: "&'Workload Planner'!C126&" volumes across "&'Hardware Inputs'!C8&" nodes. "&IF(MOD('Workload Planner'!C126,'Hardware Inputs'!C8)=0,"✓ Multiple of node count.","Consider adjusting for balance.")

C115.computed: Keep pool utilization below 70% to ensure enough free space for automatic drive failure repair. Current: 0.0%.

C116.computed: ✓ Healthy headroom for drive failure repair.

C117.value: ="Default provisioning: "&'Hardware Inputs'!C13&". Fixed (thick) is STRONGLY recommended for production. Thin risks pool exhaustion — see Thin Provisioning tab."
C117.computed: Default provisioning: Fixed (Thick). Fixed (thick) is STRONGLY recommended for production. Thin risks pool exhaustion — see Thin Provisioning tab.

C118.value: =IF('Hardware Inputs'!C13="Fixed (Thick)","✓ Using recommended Fixed provisioning.","⚠ Thin provisioning detected — monitor pool usage closely. Set WAC alert at 60%.")

C119.value: ="Size VM workloads to fit on "&'Hardware Inputs'!C8-1&" of "&'Hardware Inputs'!C8&" nodes. If 1 node goes down, VMs must failover. This is CPU/RAM planning, separate from storage."
C119.computed: Size VM workloads to fit on 2 of 3 nodes. If 1 node goes down, VMs must failover. This is CPU/RAM planning, separate from storage.

C120.value: ="Plan for "&'Hardware Inputs'!C8-1&"-node compute capacity even though storage spans all "&'Hardware Inputs'!C8&" nodes."
C120.computed: Plan for 2-node compute capacity even though storage spans all 3 nodes.

C121.value: ="Maximum single volume size: 64 TB. Current largest volume: "&TEXT(MAX('Workload Planner'!C14,'Workload Planner'!C23,'Workload Planner'!C54,'Workload Planner'!C70,'Workload Planner'!C87,'Workload Planner'!C102,'Workload Planner'!C109),"0.00")&" TB."
C121.computed: Maximum single volume size: 64 TB. Current largest volume: 0.00 TB.

C122.value: =IF(MAX('Workload Planner'!C14,'Workload Planner'!C23,'Workload Planner'!C54,'Workload Planner'!C70,'Workload Planner'!C87,'Workload Planner'!C102,'Workload Planner'!C109)>64,"⚠ WARNING: A volume exceeds 64 TB! Split into smaller volumes.","✓ All volumes within 64 TB limit.")

E129.value: ="("&'Hardware Inputs'!C42&" GB reserved for host OS)"

B3.value: Same total raw capacity — different drive counts. See how reserve and available capacity change.

E9.value: This stays constant across all alternatives.

B24.value: More drives per node = smaller drive size = smaller reserve. The reserve is always 1 drive per node (max 4), so smaller drives mean less capacity "locked up" for repair. The trade-off: more drives cost more and use more slots.

B25.value: Note: All alternatives maintain the same total raw capacity. Drive sizes shown are calculated (raw ÷ total drives) — actual drives must be purchased in available sizes (e.g., 1.6, 3.2, 3.84, 7.68 TB).

B5.value: Fixed allocates full footprint at creation. Guarantees capacity. STRONGLY recommended for production.

C14.value: Pool full = volumes OFFLINE = VMs crash. HARD STOP.

C15.value: Logical can exceed physical. No hard block.

C16.value: Updates, snapshots consume GBs with no warning.

C17.value: Stretched clusters: TRIM disabled. Volumes only grow.

C18.value: Thin S2D + thin VHDX = two overcommit layers.

C19.value: No pool space = rebuild fails = data at risk.

C22.value: Set threshold to 60% for thin pools.

C25.value: Log Analytics for pool free space trending.

B38.value: 📋 Already created volumes? Use the Volume Health Check tab to compare what you built vs. what's recommended. It calculates reserve loss, resiliency risk, and gives you resize commands.

B2.value: Compare what your volumes SHOULD be vs. what was actually created. Identifies reserve loss and resiliency risk.

B10.value: WHAT WAS ACTUALLY CREATED (enter your values below)

D11.value: Tip: Check WAC → Storage → Volumes for count

B12.value: Size of Each Volume (enter the number from WAC)

D12.value: ="WAC/PS shows TiB. Your "&TEXT(C12,"0.00")&" TiB = "&TEXT(C12*(1024/1000)^3,"0.00")&" TB (decimal). See Rosetta Stone on Hardware Inputs."
D12.computed: WAC/PS shows TiB. Your 8.00 TiB = 8.59 TB (decimal). See Rosetta Stone on Hardware Inputs.

D13.value: Three-Way Mirror, Two-Way Mirror, or Dual Parity

D14.value: Thin or Fixed — check with Get-VirtualDisk

B15.value: Actual Data Written per Volume (from WAC)

D15.value: =IF(C15=0,"Enter data per volume from Get-VirtualDisk FootprintOnPool ÷ mirror copies.","Your "&TEXT(C15,"0.00")&" TiB = "&TEXT(C15*(1024/1000)^3,"0.00")&" TB (decimal)")

B17.value: WHAT YOU SHOULD HAVE (recommended per Microsoft best practice)

D20.value: Total pool space consumed (includes mirror copies)

D21.value: Actual data you can store across all volumes

B24.value: WHAT YOU ACTUALLY HAVE (based on your inputs above)

D25.value: Three-Way Mirror = 3 copies of every byte

D26.value: What you entered above — this is what each volume can hold

D28.value: Usable × mirror copies = actual pool consumption

D29.value: Azure Local infrastructure volume (converted to TiB)

C46.value: =IF(C14="Fixed",IF(C43="✅ YES",IF(C44="✅ YES","✅ HEALTHY","⚠️ WARNING — Reduced redundancy"),"🚨 CRITICAL — Cannot survive drive failure"),IF(E43="✅ YES",IF(E44="✅ YES","✅ HEALTHY (but at risk when full)","⚠️ WARNING — Safe now, dangerous when full"),"🚨 CRITICAL — Pool will fail when volumes fill"))

B49.value: =IF(C12<=C19,"✅ Your volumes are correctly sized. No action needed.","Shrink all "&C11&" volumes from "&TEXT(C12,"0.00")&" TiB down to "&TEXT(C19,"0.00")&" TiB each. This restores your full "&TEXT(C36,"0.00")&" TiB rebuild reserve.")
B49.computed: Shrink all 3 volumes from 8.00 TiB down to 6.50 TiB each. This restores your full 19.74 TiB rebuild reserve.

B50.value: =IF(C12<=C19,"","If each volume has less than "&TEXT(C19,"0.00")&" TiB of actual data, you can shrink live without downtime using Resize-VirtualDisk or WAC.")
B50.computed: If each volume has less than 6.50 TiB of actual data, you can shrink live without downtime using Resize-VirtualDisk or WAC.

B52.value: POWERSHELL DIAGNOSTIC COMMANDS (run on any cluster node)

B53.value: # Check storage pool total and allocated size

B54.value: Get-StoragePool S2D* | Select FriendlyName, Size, AllocatedSize, OperationalStatus, HealthStatus

B56.value: # Check each volume — size, footprint, and provisioning type

B57.value: Get-VirtualDisk | Select FriendlyName, Size, FootprintOnPool, ProvisioningType, ResiliencySettingName, OperationalStatus

B60.value: Get-PhysicalDisk | Select FriendlyName, MediaType, Size, HealthStatus, OperationalStatus

B63.value: ="Get-VirtualDisk | ForEach { Resize-VirtualDisk -FriendlyName $_.FriendlyName -Size "&TEXT(C19,"0.00")&"TB }  # Resizes all volumes to "&TEXT(C19,"0.00")&" TiB"
B63.computed: Get-VirtualDisk | ForEach { Resize-VirtualDisk -FriendlyName $_.FriendlyName -Size 6.50TB }  # Resizes all volumes to 6.50 TiB

B64.value: ⚠️ Test resize on ONE volume first. Only shrink if actual data per volume is less than the target size. Back up first.

B5.value: Manufacturers label drives in decimal TB (1 TB = 1,000,000,000,000 bytes). Windows reports in binary TiB (1 TiB = 1,099,511,627,776 bytes). A 7.68 TB drive shows as ~6.98 TiB. Plus NVMe reserves 1-3% for wear leveling. Combined: ~8% reduction.

E7.value: What S2D pool reports per drive.

E8.value: Only change if vendor provides specific formatted capacity.

B11.value: Azure Local creates an infrastructure volume during deployment (~250 GB) for system metadata. This uses the same resiliency as workload volumes, so its footprint is larger.

E14.value: Three-way mirror (3+ nodes) or two-way mirror (2 nodes).

B17.value: Windows Admin Center, PowerShell, and Disk Management show TiB (binary). This calculator uses TB (decimal). Use this table to cross-check.

B26.value: ⚠️ All formula cells on input tabs are PERMANENTLY LOCKED. If you need to override a calculated value, enter it below. The override value feeds into the system in place of the formula.

B28.value: HOW TO USE: Enter an override value in column D. Leave blank to use the normal formula. The "Active Value" column shows what the system is currently using.

E28.value: ⚠️ You accept all risk when unlocking.
